data.title: Super Micro Computer Stock: With Margins In Free Fall, Here's What Comes Next
data.description:
Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI) has had a bumpy year as the stock has struggled to recover from governance concerns following delayed regulatory filings and a probe from the Department of Justice. Shares have swung wildly, only to end up in around the same place as they were 12 months prior. This leaves investors to wonder what 2026 has in store. I continue to see SMCI as a dead weight stock in a burgeoning AI market.

In my last piece on SMCI nearly 8 months ago, I recommended against the stock as declining revenue, contracting margins, and ongoing concerns about company management weighed on investor sentiment. Since that was published, shares have gained about 15%, which is well below its peers, the NASDAQ (QQQ), and AI stocks as a whole. That article can be read here.

Let's start with the preliminary Q1 2026 financial results that Super Micro released Thursday morning. While the details were sparse, the company provided some revenue projections and other tidbits that the market apparently didn't like considering SMCI is down 8% as I write this.

Here's what amounts to basically the whole release:

  • Recent design wins in excess of $12B, requesting delivery in the second quarter of fiscal year 2026 (Q2 ’26). Company will provide further updates on our upcoming earnings call on expected Q2 '26 deliveries and revenues.
  • Design win upgrades pushed some expected Q1'26 revenue to Q2 '26, resulting in estimated revenue in Q1 '26 of $5B versus $6B-$7B guidance.
  • Seeing robust demand for Supermicro Nvidia GB300, B300, RTX Pro, AMD 355X LC, now starting to ship.

The headline here, in my opinion, is the quite massive miss on revenue -- expectations for $5 billion in Q1 is more than 20% lower at the midpoint than previous guidance and analyst consensus estimates. The explanation given for this miss is that some of this previously projected revenue isn't gone but was simply pushed to Q2 due to "design win upgrades." This is not the first time Super Micro has missed on revenue guidance over the last few quarters, which is pretty dire in this booming AI market.

However, what continues to be the sticking point in the bear thesis for me is the inability of the company to generate any sort of operating leverage. As revenue has scaled, profit margins have collapsed and continue to decline, indicating that either the market is too competitive or that Super Micro has not set its product offerings apart. Below is a chart showing revenue vs. profit margin over the last three years:

This should be setting off alarm bells in every investor's brain. Part of the reason this pattern is taking place, in my opinion, is that Super Micro's regulatory concerns have hurt its ability to truly ramp up its operations as potential customers might be wary to commit to large contracts and securing funding has been difficult due to the delayed filings and governance issues. In February of last year, the company agreed on terms to borrow cash via private placement of convertible bonds, which is not typically the borrowing you see from companies with a super stable balance sheet and underlines these funding issues.

And then again in August, just a couple months ago, Super Micro filed a form 10-K with the SEC stating their internal controls were inadequate. Here's the gist:

We have concluded that our internal control over financial reporting was not effective as of June 30, 2025 due to the existence of material weaknesses in such controls.

We also cannot assure you that additional material weaknesses in our internal control over financial reporting will not arise or be identified in the future.

Not only is the company suffering from material weakness in its income statement and its ability to scale operations, Super Micro, by its own admission, cannot guarantee the accuracy of its internal controls or financial statements. What exactly is the bull thesis here? That none of the governance issues will end up mattering, the company will be able to turn its operations around, and suddenly find margins in a market that's a race to the bottom on cost with, apparently, no economies of scale?

As far as I can see, the stock is a coin flip at best, not an investment. Management has so far demonstrated an inability to regain investor trust and continues to over-promise and under-deliver. In its preliminary Q1 report, Super Micro reiterated guidance of $33 billion for FY2026, which some quick math tells us would require $28 billion in the next three quarters alone. Will the company be able to suddenly go from $5 billion in Q1 to an average of more than $9 billion in every remaining quarter in FY2026? Consider me highly skeptical.

Investor Takeaway

Though it has been almost three quarters since my last article on SMCI, my thesis remains largely unchanged -- declining operating leverage, slowing revenue growth, and governance issues make the stock one to avoid. In a market where AI stocks are surging left and right on increasing revenue, strong margins, and long-term potential, I simply don't see the value in parking capital in this dead weight investment.

In the short-term, I expect SMCI to, at best, hover around the $50 range with potential for downside as the company, in my opinion, tracks behind its annual revenue guidance. In the medium term and over the long term, I try to avoid companies that struggle to scale their operations. Revenue growth is bottoming out and margins have fallen precipitously, which could mean a rather unpleasant reckoning in the quarters ahead if Super Micro has to erode margins further to maintain that growth. As such, I am reiterating a Sell on SMCI.

Thanks for reading!

data.title: LendingClub: As Deposits Grow, Profits Are Soaring (Upgrade)
data.description:
With the stock market looking frothy, I continue to encourage investors to look outside the Magnificent 7 (or for that matter, investing in the S&P 500 as a whole because it's so dominated by the top tech stocks), and to diversify by allocating more of our portfolios toward better-value small- and mid-cap stocks.

LendingClub (NYSE:LC), in my view, is a stock that has looked increasingly attractive. The consumer lending company has pivoted successfully into becoming more of a full-service bank, and its access to deposits has allowed the company to boost its net interest margins. Shares of LendingClub have shot up ~10% after reporting Q3 results, bringing immediate gains to a stock that has seen relatively sleepy trading patterns over the past year.

I last wrote a "Neutral" article on LendingClub in 2021, when the stock was trading in the low teens. The stock has underperformed the S&P 500's rise in that timeframe. However, I now view LendingClub as a much improved company, and one that still possesses the originations growth of a much younger startup. I'm raising my rating on the stock to a "Buy."

To me, these are all the core reasons to be long on LendingClub:

  • Strong originations growth. The company has been achieving 30%+ originations growth. We note as well that it has been successful at selling the large majority of these originations off, and holding barely over 20% of its new origination on its balance sheet, freeing up capital for further originations and de-risking its operations.
  • Deposit growth is lowering LendingClub's funding costs and boosting its net interest margins. The company offers attractive high-yield savings (at 4.20% APY, as of the time of writing) and checking accounts, completing its vision of becoming more of a full-service bank. The availability of lower-cost deposits (versus more expensive capital sources earlier on in LendingClub's history) has boosted its net interest margins and allowed for tremendous profit expansion.
  • High servicing margins. LendingClub also now services loans on behalf of other institutions, which makes up over half of its servicing portfolio and gives the company access to a high-margin, zero-risk recurring revenue stream.
  • Focus on efficiency. Over the past several years, LendingClub has undergone several rounds of layoffs that have made its corporate structure much leaner, allowing for incredible profit expansion.

Stay long here: In my view, LendingClub's post-Q3 rally is just the beginning of a longer upward correction for this stock.

Q3 download

Let's now go through LendingClub's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

LendingClub's revenue grew 32% y/y to $266.2 million, which beat Wall Street's expectations of $256.0 billion (+27% y/y) by a five-point margin. We note that non-interest income, driven principally by the company's growing servicing portfolio, increased 75% y/y.

Originations growth continues to be a large part of the appeal of this stock. LendingClub's consumer loan originations grew 37% y/y to $2.6 billion. As shown below, the company is only planning on holding 23% of these originations for investment, with the rest either already sold or earmarked for sale: down 4 points y/y, and reducing the company's balance sheet risk. And also as shown, the company is expecting a similar originations growth rate between 35%-41% y/y for Q4.

The company notes that a technology advantage is what is driving strong originations growth, as LendingClub can offer loans with more attractive terms than its peers while maintaining high credit quality. Per CEO Scott Sanborn's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

With competitive loan rates enabled by our sophisticated credit models and a fast, frictionless process, we continue to be very successful at attracting our target customers. In fact, when our loan offers are made side-by-side in a leading loan comparison site, we closed 50% more customers on average than the competition.

We continue to be disciplined in our underwriting. Our asset yield remains strong and our borrower base continues to perform well. In fact, we're delivering our originations growth while also demonstrating roughly 40% outperformance on credit versus our competitor set.

Consistent strong credit performance on a high-yielding asset class has allowed us to confidently build our balance sheet, which now stands at over $11 billion, delivering a durable, resilient revenue stream that nonbanks can't replicate. In fact, this quarter, we generated our highest ever net interest income of $158 million, enabled by a growing balance sheet and expanding net interest margin.

Healthy net interest spreads are also a key part of this story. LendingClub's expansion of its checking and savings products has allowed the company to fund a greater portion of its held-for-investment loans with low-cost deposits, and the company's net interest margins in Q3 expanded 55bps y/y to 6.18%:

This gives us tremendous confidence in LendingClub's ability to maintain high net interest income even in the wake of interest rate declines. We should also pay attention to LendingClub's improving efficiency ratios, which are the result of the company's consistent attention to rightsizing its workforce. Total expenses as a percentage of net revenue fell 640bps y/y to 61.1%:

Meanwhile, GAAP net income essentially tripled y/y to $44.3 million, with EPS of $0.37 beating Wall Street's expectations of $0.31 with 19% upside.

Valuation and key takeaways

For next year FY26, Wall Street analysts are expecting LendingClub to hit $1.14 billion in total revenue (+14% y/y) and generate $1.50 in EPS (+37% y/y). This puts LendingClub's current valuation at just 12.3x FY26 P/E.

For a company that is currently growing revenue and earnings at a >30% clip, this is quite a discount in an expensive stock market. Historically, investors have viewed LendingClub as risky, with choppy execution trends. I'd argue now, however, that with the company boosting its net interest margin spreads and holding a smaller percentage of its loan originations on its balance sheet heavily de-risks this company.

Stay long here and keep riding the recent upside higher.

data.title: Worldline: Some Signs Of Stabilization, But Turnaround Protracted
data.description:
Worldline SA (OTCPK:WWLNF) (Euronext Paris: WLN) is still dealing with challenges. While there isn't an expectation of much more offboarding of high-risk business from this point onwards, and the merchant business overall is expected to be in an alright spot despite what will be lingering challenges with terminals, the bigger issue is the outlook for the financial services segment. After lapping tough comps, there is expected to be continued pressure on the business. There may also be a need for some investments to industrialize KYC, which could be a factor for the EBITDA guidance.

But other than that, things look to be stabilizing a bit, though the recovery won't be particularly quick. The cash pooling system that they use has also been addressed. The outstanding questions are the money laundering probe in Belgium, and solicitations are apparently occurring in Sweden in some audits. It's still a volatile issue and a battleground. It's risky, but we're already in it, and we want to wait, given the protracted turnaround.

Latest Results

The big news that markets responded to, other than some signs of operational stabilization, is the narrowing profit forecasts.

It is quite important for investors to be sensitive to this because it says quite a lot about the state of their HBR businesses, which are more involved and carry much higher margins. Indeed, the offboarding process is apparently nearing its conclusion for HBR. Management emphasized that all their peers also have some markets like this and that it is normal for a company of this scale.

The merchant business is sequentially stabilizing. Germany is a bit of an issue, but some of the JV-based businesses are driving a decent situation in other European geographies. The terminal businesses are still not doing well due to the fact that their terminals, due to supply issues, are not hitting the market quite in time for the upgrade cycles. So, this is a bit of a protracted issue, and margins are quite good for this business. That means a negative mix effect, and it is driving some churn that will need effort to be reversed in the future.

For the FS segment, card-based payments processing was stable, and there was some strength in upselling contracts and volume increases on existing customers. However, account payment activities brought down the segment. There are apparently not that many projects in the pipeline at the moment, and it will take a bit to restart the transformational projects that are a shortfall for the moment in the segment results. Also, some related milestones are less than guaranteed, also pressuring this part of the segment.

Bottom Line

There are still going to be some revenue pressures, which is why the revenue guidance hasn't narrowed. EBITDA guidance has narrowed, but mix effects are still negative and in play, and MSV growth hasn't been that great given macro softness in Europe.

On the cash pooling issue, there were concerns around the parent company making use of overdrafts as a liquidity source where credit facilities and other liquidity sources are more dependable. But the company explained the notional pooling concept, where subsidiaries don't need as much cash as they produce for working capital requirements and therefore can offer it at the parent level to deal with parent-level debts.

We are not sure how exotic such a structure is, but it's good that it's being scrutinized. The company has been using this system for 10 years, apparently. There is no co-mingling of merchant and corporate cash, according to management in the earnings call.

Disposals also continue. We mentioned the MeTS sale being at a slightly higher multiple than the broader business. They also sold their U.S. businesses, which include Bambora, a payment gateway that Shift4 is buying. It's at close to a 10x EV/EBITDA multiple, but only about 1% of forecast EBITDA (though 3% of the current EV). They are signaling more deals to come, likely to leave some geographies. It is a better environment right now for European dealmaking, so we don't see this as a badly timed capitulation.

In terms of valuation, the 3.15x EV/EBITDA is low relative to other payment peers like Nexi (OTCPK:NEXPF) at 5.7x. There is clearly a trust issue in Worldline, and they are underperforming peers due to this offboarding process and the issues in terminals. There are protracted issues that could take another year to resolve and drive some renewed positive performance. Other than maybe some more deals to come and the resolution of some of the outstanding probes, no major catalysts. The less commodified front-end segment should turn around in a year or two, and FS should also stabilize. The industry is still growing, and the compressed price may be a basis for some recovery, given not too much more bad news and a clearer outlook that will limit fuel for this quite crowded short.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Montauk Renewables Is Not Risky Now, But Leverage Is Adding Up
data.description:
Montauk Renewables, Inc. (NASDAQ:MNTK) is a producer of renewable natural gas (RNG), primarily from landfills.

The company enjoyed a period of profitability up to very recently, but oversupply in the RNG market has led to the company not being able to cover its operational or interest costs on a GAAP basis, although on a cash basis the situation is not that bad.

The situation is not expected to change in the short term, mainly because of regulatory factors. In this context, the company is hoping to pivot to other forms of renewable gas, adding capacity in animal-based gases, at a large capital cost, with untested economics.

Considering all of this, I believe the operational and mid-term financial risks around MNTK are high, and therefore believe the name is not attractive for investment at a 10x adjusted-CFO multiple. For that reason, I initiate with a Hold.

Renewable Gas and Electricity from Landfills

Montauk generates renewable energy from the gas released by different waste resources. Today, the majority of its operations are based on landfill waste (other forms are animal waste).

When organic matter decomposes inside a landfill (or a biodigester containing animal feces or urine), it generates gases. One of them is methane (CH4), the main molecule of natural gas.

The gases can be burned to move a turbine and generate electricity. This is the simplest method for utilizing that gas, requiring less purification. Montauk has two landfill operations generating renewable electricity in this way, for a total capacity of about 27 MW (10-K under 'Sites').

The gases can also be treated to yield methane without other elements (such as nitrogen and CO2), which is indistinguishable from regular natural gas. This gas can be injected into the gas network or sold for use as vehicle fuel (compressed natural gas), primarily by mid-distance trucks. The company has 11 RNG operations, for a total capacity of 33 thousand MMBTU (million British Thermal Unit, a natural gas energy unit) per day, plus an additional expansion coming for another 2 thousand (10-K under Sites and MD&A).

The Role of Subsidies or 'Attributes'

Even though it comes from garbage, using the gases from landfills or husbandry waste is not cheap. The resulting energy or natural gas is not remotely competitive with regular natural gas energy.

However, the use of these gases results in very important environmental services. Of utmost importance is avoiding the release of methane to the atmosphere. Methane is much more polluting than CO2. By separating that waste and burning it in a controlled fashion, the release is CO2. In addition, by replacing a fuel source of that same methane, one avoids the release of those additional CO2 units.

Therefore, the US federal government or states mandate that electricity companies and oil and gas companies purchase some renewable energy or natural gas from waste. In addition, the governments allow a certain level of 'credits' to be generated by gas treatment companies like Montauk, which can be sold for a price. These are called 'Environmental Attributes' on the 10-K.

In the case of electricity, the most important credit/subsidy is the Renewable Energy Certificate, or REC, and in the case of RNG, the equivalent credits are called Renewable Identification Numbers, or RINs, at the federal level and Low Carbon Fuel Standards (LCFS) in California (specifically important for husbandry waste operations).

The prices of these credits are not determined by a market but rather by regulators (the EPA federally or state regulators locally). In addition, the regulators determine how many credits will be available in a given year, therefore managing the incentive to build these types of facilities.

To give an idea of the importance of credits, the price of 1 MMBTU of natural gas is $4, but it can generate about 11 RINs, each of which was sold for $2.4 in Q2 2025. It means 1 MMBTU of RNG generates about $4 for its energy content at market and at least $26.5 from its credit value.

According to Montauk's 10-K, 75% of its revenues in FY 2024 came from the sale of Environmental Attributes (credits/subsidies).

Falling Margins

Until very recently, Montauk had profitable operations (the company went public in 2021; therefore, previous years are backfilled).

However, since 2023, the margins of the business have been decreasing. As of Q2 2025 and H1 2025 (see Q2 2025 10-Q), the company is operationally unprofitable.

At the center of this are two dynamics.

First, the EPA, at least under the new federal administration, is decreasing the quota of RINs that it requires from oil and gas companies. According to the company's latest earnings call, the EPA also believes the RNG market will be oversupplied because it does not expect the transport market of CNG to grow as much as RNG projects. There might be political drivers behind this (lowering the cost for oil and gas companies), but there is also a driver from oversupply, or at least some excess of RNG projects.

The second factor is also related to this oversupply, but more to the lack of feedstock and to the power of feedstock owners, i.e., landfill owners. Montauk already generates 63% of its revenues from landfills owned by two companies: Waste Management, Inc. (WM) and Republic Waste. On its 10-K (in the risk section), the company also clarifies that out of the 38 remaining landfills (in the US) where the company could operate commercially, 25 are owned by these two companies. What this generates is competition for the supply, giving power to the controllers of that supply. They can request a higher payment for using the landfill gas, or otherwise they can contract with another company or even run the processing themselves. Today, royalties (which capture this cost) are already 20% of the company's revenues and growing in 1H25.

All of the above lead to lower margins, and these drivers do not seem to be going to disappear, at least in the short to medium term.

Pivoting to Husbandry Waste

In this context, the company has one expansion landfill RNG project to be completed and two listed that will have completion in 2027 (and could potentially be delayed if conditions warrant that).

The company's largest capital expense plan is concentrated in another source of gases, swine farms in North Carolina (called the Ag Renewable project). The gas will be used to generate electricity, not RNG.

This is a different animal (no pun intended) because the capital requirement is much larger (about $200 million versus $30/40 million for a landfill one, according to the company's latest presentation) and also more complex. Instead of having a single source of gas (a landfill), the company will need to connect its processing facilities to several swine farms and install equipment on each of them.

The economics of this business are also untested, as the company manages a single livestock RNG facility in Idaho, and it is its smallest RNG plant.

Financing Operations Is Not In Trouble, But No Financing For CapEx

The company is operationally and net income unprofitable because of D&A. If we removed this component, the company would actually remain CFO positive.

FCFs are negative because of significant CapEx into expansion. The company already records maintenance as an operational expense (meaning it should be included in the CFO above) but also spends some CAPEX on maintenance and not growth. How much CapEx is maintenance CapEx is not clear, but in FY 2024 and FY 2023 (see cash flows in the FY 2024 10-K), the part of CapEx that was not specifically accounted for in one project was $13 million and $18 million, respectively. This could be a proxy for maintenance CapEx, leading to an adjusted CFO of about $30 million.

The above means, in my opinion, that the company is not going to operationally face cash problems. However, it cannot finance the continued expansion of its production at current levels from its own operations. It has required so far the expansion of its net debt levels, which again are not unsustainable but are not self-funded either.

Too Uncertain For A Buy At These Prices

Today, although still financially profitable from a cash perspective on operations, the company's margins are decreasing, and according to my analysis from filings and recent calls, that trend will continue.

In this context, the company is aggressively leveraging, as seen above, to finance expansion projects. The largest of those projects, equivalent to four to five of the others, is in a subsegment of its industry where the company has little experience, and that may not have the economics expected of it. Further, it may prove harder to ramp up than believed, which can again have an impact on cash availability.

In this context, Montauk trades for about $312 million in market cap. Compared to that, an adjusted CFO (based on a $15 million maintenance CapEx proxy) of $30 million does not seem that bad.

However, considering the decreasing margins, increasing leverage, and the potential uncertainty from the completion and ramp-up of a large project in an unknown segment, I do not believe the company is sufficiently discounted.

For that reason, I initiate coverage of the name with a Hold rating.

data.title: Tesla Still Has Mileage Despite Recent Headwinds
data.description:
I've made two important calls on Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) for 2025, with a Sell rating in December 2024 that was followed by a 50% drop in the stock. Then, there was a Buy recommendation in March that is now up 70%, ahead of the S&P 500 (SP500) benchmark's gain of almost 19%. I'm going to buck the trend again and remain bullish on Tesla as investors grow increasingly fearful.

Q3 earnings miss has investors running

A quick look at fellow Seeking Alpha analysts' calls on Tesla shows there is a very bearish tone. The company now has a Sell rating amongst analysts, alongside Hold ratings from Wall Street and the Quant ratings.

The reason for the latest bearish turn has been driven by the third quarter earnings miss and underlying sales dynamics. Tesla was able to deliver a record number of vehicles in Q3, but this was helped by the end of EV tax credits carried over from the President Joe Biden administration.

Tesla's delivery of 497,000 vehicles in Q3 was a strong jump from the Q1 figure of 336,681. In days gone by, that would've been enough to give the stock a boost, but there was a negative reaction to the earnings release. I believe this is the first positive sign that investors are currently discounting.

I believe that the negative reaction to Tesla is being caused by the belief that there is an "all-or-nothing" bet in play on the company's future sales.

Seeking Alpha noted an Underweight rating at Wells Fargo, with analysts seeing "the core business deteriorating and with robotaxi/Optimus seen as taking longer to scale than anticipated."

However, I see the underlying core business as being a mixed bag of strong top line performance, alongside some strained profitability metrics. Total revenue at Tesla is up 12% year-over-year, and it is obviously not ideal to see a 40% decline year-over-year in income from operations.

However, the income from operations figure was higher than all of the last three quarters and led to a record free cash flow figure of almost $4 billion.

Alongside this, Tesla had a cash position of $41,6 billion, up 24% year-over-year. If Tesla is looking to embark on a push for new revenue streams, it is doing so with an enviable war chest.

Tesla's price-to-cash flow is still high at 274x, according to MacroTrends data, but investors were largely bullish on the company in December 2024 at above 400x.

If we take the robotaxi market as an example, WeRide (WRD) reported a cash and equivalents position of $570.6 million in Q2. All autonomous vehicle firms are currently in the testing phase and setting up overseas partnerships. But when the green light is switched on, Tesla's readymade fleet will dwarf anything that its rivals can do. CEO Elon Musk stated this in the recent earnings call:

"We have millions of cars out there that with a software update become full self-driving cars. And we're making a couple of million a year."

I also believe that investors should give Tesla some credit for what has been a tumultuous year. Elon Musk was otherwise engaged at the White House at the beginning of the year, while March brought the the U.S. tariff shock.

Tesla's CFO Vaibhav Taneja said the tariff hit to the company had been around $400 million.

Updates on new revenue streams

During the Q2 earnings call, Musk gave an update on the robotaxi theme.

He highlighted the company's cautious approach, saying that robotaxi rides should begin in Austin before the end of the year, and also be active in 8 or 10 metro areas. As testing continues over that period, it is not inconceivable to start expecting a rapid scaling from early 2026.

“We’re at a critical inflection point for Tesla and our strategy going forward as we bring AI into the real world,” Musk said. Noting the company was at the “beginning of scaling, quite massively, Full Self-Driving and Robotaxi, and fundamentally changing the nature of transport.”

Musk also provided an update on the Optimus robot, saying, "We look forward to unveiling Optimus V3 in Q1, 2026. It won't seem like a robot but a human in a suit."

That timeframe could also bring a more accurate picture on commercial production of the robots, but Musk said it could become "the biggest product of all time."

As I noted earlier, Tesla has actually delivered a strong year on deliveries and that ties in further to the robotaxi revenue theme. But, there were signs that production was about to step higher.

"At this point, I feel like we've got clarity, and it makes sense to expand production as fast as we reasonably can," Musk said.

That clarity could be driven by the fact that the company saw a rebound in sales across its geographical locations in Q2, with China and APAC up 33% and 29%, North America up 28%, and EMEA up 25%.

Another positive for Tesla was a reversal in fortunes in the Chinese market. The company was on the slide, with domestic rival BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) surpassing it in sales. However, a Chinese government crackdown on "disorderly" competition led to BYD's first-ever year-on-year sales drop in Q3 since 2020.

In the third quarter of 2025, the company experienced its first year-on-year drop in total sales since 2020, alongside a 30% drop in net income.

Tesla's energy storage division was another bright spot with a 44% revenue improvement year-over-year.

Risks to the investment thesis

Tesla is obviously trading at a high valuation, but that has been the case for many years. While I saw dark clouds looming in December 2024, due to Musk's alignment with President Trump, that has now been digested. There are now green shoots in the Chinese market. Tesla has delivered a strong year with record deliveries, record cash flow, and a strong cash position of more than $40 billion.

As Elon Musk has noted, the move to robotaxi revenue across millions of vehicles starts with a software upgrade. That should be the key focus for investors as testing of the technology moves ahead into the year-end.

The U.S.-China trade negotiations are a big risk to consider, but recent comments from Trump have suggested that an agreement can be made.

Thursday brought a potential headwind to the auto sector with talk of chip shortages related to the Dutch seizure of Chinese firm Nexperia. Production appears to be back on track as I write, and the U.S. and China relations are again important to these types of headwinds.

Conclusion

While Tesla trades near record highs, there has been a move to a cautious tone among many analysts. I believe that could be the setup for a surprise move higher in the stock. I went against the crowd ahead of 2025, and caught the lows in March, and I am happy to hold on here. I still don't see this as the end of the road for Tesla and, while Wall Street analysts appear to be getting impatient over the company's future, clarity could come as soon as Q1 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Silver Rebounds Above $49 Amid Geopolitical Tensions
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

​Silver prices climbed back above $49 an ounce on Thursday, recovering from one of their sharpest weekly declines in years as investors re-entered the market amid renewed geopolitical volatility. The rebound comes after silver tumbled more than 8% earlier in the week, its steepest one-day drop since 2021, leaving the metal nearly 10% below its recent record highs.

The U.S. government’s decision to blacklist Russian oil giants Rosneft and Lukoil sent ripples through commodity markets, injecting renewed volatility into energy and metals trading. The move, which effectively targets nearly half of Russia’s oil exports, reignited concerns over global supply disruptions and prompted fresh flows into safe-haven assets such as silver and gold.

Simultaneously, Washington’s reported consideration of software export restrictions to China revived broader trade tension fears, though President Donald Trump sought to temper market anxiety by confirming his scheduled meeting with Chinese President Xi Jinping. The confluence of these developments has reinforced silver’s dual appeal—as both a defensive hedge against uncertainty and a key industrial metal with growing long-term demand from green technology sectors.

While silver’s prior rally was partly fueled by speculative momentum, underlying fundamentals remain supportive. Tightness in London and Shanghai vault inventories continues to highlight constrained supply, while demand growth from solar panel production, electric vehicles, and advanced electronics underpins the structural case for strength.

Technical structure points to a fragile rebound

From a technical standpoint, silver’s recovery remains cautious but encouraging. On the 4-hour chart, prices have rebounded from support just below $48, reclaiming ground toward the 20-period exponential moving average (EMA) near $49.47. The move signals an attempt by buyers to stabilize sentiment following heavy liquidation earlier in the week.

However, the broader uptrend channel that guided silver higher through September and early October has been broken, underscoring the market’s vulnerability. The immediate resistance zone lies at $50–$50.40, defined by the 50-period EMA and the lower boundary of the former channel. A sustained close above this range would signal renewed bullish momentum and open the door to a retest of $52–$53. Failure to clear it could see prices revisit $47.10 (100-period EMA) and possibly $45.50, with longer-term support at $43 aligned with the rising trendline from July.

Momentum indicators show tentative improvement but remain far from confirming a full recovery. The Relative Strength Index (RSI) has lifted off oversold territory, while volume analysis indicates short-covering rather than aggressive new buying.

Outlook: recovery hinges on $50 breakout

As discussed in previous analyses, silver remains fundamentally supported by the intersection of safe-haven flows and industrial demand growth. The $50 mark now stands as the critical pivot point—its defense or breach likely to determine the next major directional move. A breakout above $50 would likely attract momentum buyers and validate a recovery phase toward the $52–$53 region. Conversely, failure to sustain above that ceiling could extend the consolidation and expose the metal to another correction, particularly if macro sentiment shifts back toward risk assets.

In the bigger picture, silver retains its bullish foundation, but with volatility high and profit-taking still active, the road ahead may remain uneven. Traders will closely watch geopolitical headlines and Friday’s U.S. inflation data for cues on the next decisive move.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Why Union Pacific Could Be One Of The Best Investments We Can Make Right Now
data.description:
Union Pacific - An Introduction

If there's one company I have brought up a lot this year, it's Union Pacific Corporation (NYSE:UNP), which is one of the first five dividend growth stocks I bought in 2020 when I decided to get serious about building a long-term portfolio of high-quality stocks that consistently grow their payout.

However, that's not the main reason I bring it up so often. The main reason is that we are dealing with America's largest Class I railroad, which not only provides investors with a lot of value but also tells us a lot about the economy and can be used as a proxy for cyclical growth.

See, on top of currently covering the entire Western two-thirds in a duopoly with Buffett-owned BNSF, it is currently waiting for approval for a merger with Norfolk Southern, which would create the first transcontinental railroad in the United States, a giant connecting every economic hotspot that could, as dramatic as it may sound, have a very positive impact on the U.S. economy.

As I have written in my most recent article on this giant (July 29), I believe the merger will get approved. For now, that statement looks good, as even the president has made the case that he's very much in favor of it.

Since my last article, UNP shares have lost roughly 2%, lagging the S&P 500 by more than 700 basis points. In fact, including dividends, this Nebraska-based railroad has been dead money since early 2022. That's why the ten-year total return is just 190%, below the market's 285% total return (see below).

The important thing to mention here is that this isn't because UNP is a bad railroad. It's because, as I just mentioned, UNP is very cyclical. To provide some proof, I made the chart below, which shows both the year-over-year performance of UNP's stock price and the leading ISM Manufacturing Index.

As I am very bullish on cyclical growth, I believe UNP is primed for a rebound similar to 2016 and 2021, which resulted in triple-digit longer-term returns.

Now, as Union Pacific has just released its earnings, I am using this opportunity to update my thesis and give you a very detailed breakdown of why I am so very bullish on the company that was founded when Abraham Lincoln was still in office.

So, let's dive in!

A Performance That's Truly Noteworthy

Cyclical growth may have been a mess. However, Union Pacific still performed well, driven by Jim Vena, who became CEO in 2023. As Bloomberg reported a few months ago, he improved everything, except for total carload volumes, which is something that only macro improvements can meaningfully impact, given the size of this railway operation:

Under Vena’s actions, which included closing switching yards and parking locomotives, Union Pacific’s operating profit margin rose from about 37% in 2018 to almost 43% in 2021. During his two-year stint as chief operating officer, Vena helped Union Pacific save $1 billion, Fritz said at the time. Returns to shareholders jumped. What didn’t increase was carload volume. - Bloomberg

Since 2022, we have even had a freight/trucking recession, as headwinds from post-pandemic consumer weakness, sticky inflation, rising rates, a stuck housing market, and tariffs were simply too much.

Despite the annoying fact that we're still in a cyclical rut, so to speak, Union Pacific has done well. So, allow me to bombard you with some numbers.

For starters, the company achieved six consecutive quarters of growth in freight revenue excluding fuel surcharges. These numbers rose to a new record of $5.3 billion. That's 4% more compared to the prior-year quarter.

Fuel surcharges dropped by 5%, which is due to lower energy prices, as the company isn't billing its customers more for higher fuel prices, as it is encountering lower costs. As a result, freight revenue was up 3%. That's decent, especially because operating costs rose by only 1%.

At this point, one doesn't need an MBA to know that this means operating leverage improved, as it generated 6% higher operating income. This lowered the operating ratio to less than 60.0%. In this environment of poor cyclical growth and sticky inflation, it's a number I would be screaming from the rooftops if I were the CEO. That's how good it is. No peer comes close (slide 10).

Impressively, the company boosted freight revenues by 3% while lowering compensation by 1%, as a lower employee count more than offset wage inflation. While I will never celebrate lower employee levels, this shows that the company's operations are indeed extremely resilient and efficient.

Based on this context, we see that volumes were quite good in the quarter. While premium (that's cars and consumer-related intermodal containers) offset gains in other areas, both bulk and industrial goods saw higher volumes.

This includes higher petrochemical volumes, partially provided by the company's aggressive investments in boosting its footprint in high-demand markets like the Gulf Coast:

We're positioned to finish strong in our petrochemicals market. That's driven by the investments we've made in our Gulf Coast franchise and the strength of our service product, which continues to help us win with new customers. In fact, we recently won new petrochemical business that began earlier this month. - UNP 3Q25 Earnings Call

Unfortunately consumer weakness and the trade war have kept intermodal volumes down, as the company suffered from lower West Coast imports. Note that Southern California is one of its biggest markets, which is also home to the San Pedro Port Complex, the biggest port system in the United States.

Overall, intermodal volumes were challenged by [Lower of West Coast] imports, resulted in a 17% decrease in international volumes. - UNP 3Q25 Earnings Call

With that said, one major issue Union Pacific had before Jim Vena took over was a very poor operating performance, as it had become unreliable and unable to keep up with volatile supply chains. Note that back in 2021 and 2022, the rail market was hit by massive volume volatility, as the U.S. economy reopened, which was quickly followed by high inflation and consumers reducing spending. It was a nightmare for their operations.

In 3Q25, it reached 98% service levels for intermodal and 100% for manifest operations, which are essentially combined carloads. That's fantastic. It also saw a 4% improvement in locomotive productivity, 6% higher workforce productivity (including a 4% headcount cut), and a 2% increase in average train lengths.

So, while there is no meaningful growth, the company was still able to report record efficiency gains despite shipping a record number of goods.

The company also used its earnings to explain that it achieved an industry-leading safety performance, which is important, as cutting expenses can lead to accidents. After all, cutting expenses is easy. Doing it while maintaining superior operations is the tough part.

To summarize, Union Pacific had:

  • Record shipments.
  • Record labor productivity.
  • Record train lengths.

All of this happened in a challenging environment without macro tailwinds.

The Future Of UNP And Its Shareholders

When it comes to the pending merger, the company noted that, on top of job guarantees for union jobs, it is seeing a path to substantial efficiency gains.

For the majority of our customers, it is going to improve with the speed and how many assets they're going to have to have and how fast we can move anytime anybody that crosses that today hands off.

[...] We have over 1,200 stakeholders... But if you just look at the customers, we've got over 400 customers that have sent in a letter of support and there's still a pipeline behind that. - UNP 3Q25 Earnings Call

Even better, on top of provable competition tailwinds for the U.S. economy, the company has a (pending) agreement with all unions, which means the first round of negotiations has been finished. This is critical for total progress.

With that said, Union Pacific also used the third quarter to halt buybacks due to the pending merger. It reduced total debt to less than $32.0 billion, which pushed its net leverage ratio to 2.6x, the lowest number in at least a year.

This fully supports its A-range credit rating, which the company is expected to maintain after the merger has been finalized due to its focus on debt reduction and imminent synergy gains.

It currently has a 2.5% dividend yield, protected by a payout ratio of slightly more than 40%. The dividend has been hiked for 18 consecutive years, with high-single-digit growth in recent years. This year, it hiked by just 3%, which makes sense in light of economic challenges, and because I believe it paved the road for a bigger focus on merger-related debt reduction.

Unfortunately, the company is still struggling with volumes, which is why it had some very carefully-chosen words during its earnings call about volume headwinds and the difficulties these bring for efficiency gains:

Volumes are currently running down 6% as international intermodal volumes reflect the tough comparison against last year's strong growth.

[...] Productivity, as you know, also is challenged when you have volumes coming down. And so the team accepts that challenge knows that they have that there, but that will create a little bit of a headwind.

[...] Our energy and specialized markets are expected to remain challenged, primarily driven by fewer petroleum shipments as we continue to balance volume at the right margin. - UNP 3Q25 Earnings Call

Despite these headwinds, the company expects to achieve its 3-year EPS CAGR view of high-single to low-double-digit growth, and it sees accretive pricing on top of industry-leading efficiencies.

This is the perfect foundation for the cyclical recovery I see on the horizon.

It's also the point where I show you its valuation.

Currently, the company trades at a blended P/E ratio of 19.4x, which is below its ten-year average of 21.2x. Analysts expect 8-9% annual EPS growth in 2026 and 2027, which is in line with the company's own guidance.

In other words, if we include its dividend, we get a base-case total return of slightly more than 10.0% per year, without any valuation tailwinds or growth surprises.

While risks like a recession still apply, I believe that any meaningful cyclical upswing and rebound in energy prices (fuel surcharges), I expect this company to grow its EPS by 15-20% per year, similar to prior rebounds.

For example, even without growth tailwinds, a return to its ten-year average P/E ratio could lead to 15% annual returns. That's my absolute base case for the next 2-3 years.

It's also the reason why all of our family accounts own UNP shares and why I will use future corrections to boost my already large position even further.

Takeaway

Union Pacific has been dead money since 2021. There's no sugarcoating that ugly fact.

However, despite these headwinds, its ten-year total return is still close to 200%, as the company does really well during periods of solid cyclical growth, supported by what has become extreme resilience during downturns.

As I believe we are close to a rebound in cyclical growth with a side of secular growth from energy exports, economic re-shoring, and the pending merger, I think this company has what it takes to boost its EPS by double digits in the years ahead, further grow its dividend, and potentially beat the market by a wide margin.

I'm betting on it.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk here is cyclical weakness. If I'm wrong and this economy is headed for a recession, UNP will almost certainly see more weakness. Other risks include potential derailments, labor strikes, and a blocked merger, which would result in the payment of a $2.5 billion termination fee.

data.title: Above The Noise: Has The 'Bubble Talk' Been Blown Out Of Proportion?
data.description:
There was a moment recently when the S&P 500 Index was trading at 6,700 and the 10-year US Treasury yield was at 4.10%.1 I briefly considered releasing Above the Noise right then, if only to grab the attention of the nation’s teenagers. Six seven! Forty-one!

If that makes no sense to you, ask your youngest relatives. Or I can try to explain it, though my nearly 50 years on this planet may disqualify me as a reliable interpreter. Here’s the secret: It’s part of a wave of Gen Alpha slang that’s intentionally nonsensical or confusing. And honestly, that’s probably a fitting segue into the current market narrative.

Some observers view this third consecutive year of outsized US stock gains as similarly nonsensical.2 That perception likely stems from elevated valuations,3 but valuations have never been a great timing tool. So, let’s try to make things less confusing.

The economy has been resilient. The Atlanta Federal Reserve (Fed) estimates real gross domestic product (GDP) growth is running between 3% and 4%.4 Corporate earnings have been strong.5 Inflation expectations are contained.6 Yields have fallen.7 Oil prices are below $60.8 Credit spreads are tight.9 And the Fed appears ready to cut rates further.10 In a “Goldilocks-ish” macro environment like this, I’m not inclined to fight the Fed.

Phone a friend

Let’s immediately get to the artificial intelligence (AI) bubble question. I reached out to Ash Shah, Senior Portfolio Manager of the Discovery Growth team at Invesco. His response:

“There are certain echoes of past tech bubbles in today’s markets, particularly the AI circular investment deals, where suppliers, investors, and customers become intertwined.

There are key differences, however, that make this moment distinct from the late 1990s. The companies driving much of the AI spending today are well-capitalized, generate substantial cash flow, and have proven business models.11 Valuations, while elevated, aren’t at the extremes of the dot-com era.12 For example, NVIDIA (NVDA) trades at around 23x (times) forward earnings, compared to Cisco’s (CSCO) 80x multiple during the height of the 1990s bubble.13 Back then, fiber was being laid without a clear use case. Today, demand appears insatiable, with NVIDIA essentially reporting that it can’t ship chips fast enough.14

We do need to monitor the risks. Certain companies, for example, OpenAI (OPENAI), have committed to sizeable spending in the coming years and are relying on continued access to capital markets to support it. If the funding dries up, especially for firms that are increasingly interconnected, then we could see a meaningful unwind of stock valuations. So far, there are little to no signs of stress in the capital markets.”

It may be confirmation bias, but …

… the concerns over credit card balances and delinquencies may be overstated. Delinquency rates had been remarkably stable for years, until inflation surged in 2022. That spike in prices led to record-level credit card balances, sparking fears that consumers were overextending themselves. However, that’s a bit like saying “Avengers: Endgame” made more money than the original “Star Wars.” That’s only true if you don’t adjust for inflation. In real dollar terms, balances declined for most credit risk tiers, with the notable exception of the lowest-rated borrowers.15 Not surprisingly, as inflation has generally moderated,16 the rate of growth in credit card delinquencies has also flatlined.17

It was said

“As we saw during the Covid pandemic, lab-created experiments can wreak havoc when they escape their confines. Once released, they can’t easily be put back. The ‘extraordinary’ monetary-policy tools unleashed after the 2008 Financial Crisis have similarly transformed the Federal Reserve’s policy regime, with unpredictable consequences.”

— US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent

This one draws my ire a bit. I was there in 2008, staring into a deflationary abyss. Fiscal support was scarce, rendering monetary policy as the only game in town. For a decade afterward, the Fed struggled to get inflation expectations back up into the comfort zone.18 When the Fed raised rates in 2015, the dollar surged19 and the stock market didn’t handle it well.20

I was also there in 2020, during the pandemic, facing yet another deflationary threat. Again, the Fed responded forcefully. Inflation did emerge, but fiscal spending and supply chain disruptions played a major role. Now, five years later, we’ve come through with inflation expectations contained21 and a 4.3% unemployment rate.22 That’s no small feat given the magnitude of those two deflationary shocks.

If anything, rather than scolding the policies of former Fed Chair Ben Bernanke, I’d support building a monument to him on the National Mall.

Since you asked (Part 1)

Q: Do you believe in the debasement trade?

A: First, let’s define the debasement trade. It’s based on the belief that fiat currencies, especially the US dollar, are losing purchasing power due to persistent government deficits and aggressive monetary policy. Investors who subscribe to this view hedge against currency erosion by shifting capital into hard assets that can’t be easily printed or diluted, such as gold, bitcoin, and other commodities.

Personally, I don’t subscribe to the debasement trade, notwithstanding the recent rises in gold and bitcoin.23 If anything, the US dollar has shown remarkable stability and has even rallied recently after falling from historically lofty levels earlier in the year.24 There’s been a strong appetite for US Treasuries, with 10-year yields falling nearly 80 basis points this year,25 and continued demand for US stocks.26 While assets like gold and bitcoin can serve as potential hedges within a diversified portfolio, I wouldn’t advocate for a substantial allocation to the so-called debasement trade.

Since you asked (Part 2)

Q: Would the US economy be in a recession if there wasn’t a surge in AI investment?

A: That’s almost like asking if the Kansas City Chiefs would have as many championship rings without Patrick Mahomes. They have Patrick Mahomes. But seriously, the surge in AI investment is masking some underlying softness in the economy. Payroll growth is slowing,27 manufacturing surveys have hovered just below the line between expansion and contraction,28 and housing activity remains weak.29 It’s hard to prove a counterfactual, but I suspect that without the AI boom, policymakers might have already moved more aggressively to ease conditions, potentially reinvigorating housing and other interest-rate-sensitive sectors.

The point is, we do have AI investment, and it has supported aggregate growth. And policy easing appears to be continuing. So, regardless of how you attempt to prove this counterfactual, the backdrop for risk assets looks promising.

On the road again

I held back on travel this month for a variety of different reasons. My colleague took the tour bus from Pittsburgh to Detroit to Kansas City to Omaha to Baton Rouge, and other locations along the way. I’m not going to deny that I had FOMO (fear of missing out), even as I did enjoy being home for the family dinners. My world tour commences next week. Rest assured, I’ll be back with my travel notes.

Enjoy the fall. As Billie Holiday, Ella Fitzgerald, Louis Armstrong, Frank Sinatra, and many others sang, “Autumn in New York, it lifts you up.” I couldn’t agree more.

Footnotes

  • 1 Source: Bloomberg L.P., Oct. 15, 2025. On Oct. 8, 2025, the 10-year US Treasury rate traded at 4.10% before closing at 4.11%. The S&P 500 Index closed at 6,753.
  • 2 Source: Bloomberg L.P., Oct. 15, 2025, based on the returns of the S&P 500 Index, which posted returns in 2023, 2024, and year to date Oct. 14, 2025 of 26.26%, 25.00%, and 14.10%, respectively.
  • 3 Source: Bloomberg L.P., Oct. 15, 2025, based on the price-to-earnings ratio of the S&P 500 Index.
  • 4 Source: Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta, based on the Atlanta Fed GDPNow GDP Forecast.

    GDPNow is a nowcasting model created by the Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta that forecasts real GDP growth by aggregating 13 components that make up GDP with the chain-weighting methodology used by the US Bureau of Economic Analysis.

  • 5 Source: Bloomberg L.P., June 30, 2025, based on the second quarter earnings of the companies in the S&P 500 Index.
  • 6 Source: Bloomberg L.P., Oct. 15, 2025, based on the 3-year US Treasury inflation breakeven. A breakeven inflation rate is a market-derived estimate of future inflation, calculated by comparing the yield on a standard government bond (nominal) to the yield on a Treasury Inflation-Protected Security (TIPS) of the same maturity.
  • 7 Source: Bloomberg L.P., Oct. 15, 2025, based on the 10-year US Treasury rate, which peaked at 4.79% on Jan. 14, 2025 and closed at 4.03% on Oct. 14, 2025.
  • 8 Source: Bloomberg L.P., Oct. 15, 2025, based on West Texas Intermediate Crude Oil Domestic Sweet.
  • 9 Source: Bloomberg L.P., Oct. 15, 2025, based on the yield spread between the Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index and the 3-month US Treasury yield.
  • 10 Source: Bloomberg L.P., Oct. 15, 2025, based on the fed funds implied rate, which is the difference between the spot rate and the futures rate, which is an interest rate that can be calculated for any security with a futures contract.
  • 11 Comments are related to companies such as Microsoft, Alphabet, and Meta. The mention of individual names is not intended as investment advice.
  • 12 Source: Bloomberg L.P., Oct. 15, 2025, based on the price-to-earnings ratio.
  • 13 Source: Bloomberg L.P., Oct. 15, 2025.
  • 14 Source: CNBC, “Nvidia shares rise after CEO Huang says AI computing demand is up ‘substantially’” Oct. 8, 2025.
  • 15 Source: TransUnion Credit Card Industry Insights Report, 2025.
  • 16 Source: US Bureau of Economic Analysis, Aug. 31, 2025, based on the year-over-year percent change in the core Personal Consumption Expenditure. Personal consumption expenditures (PCE), or the PCE Index, measures price changes in consumer goods and services. Expenditures included in the index are actual US household expenditures. Core PCE excludes food and energy prices.
  • 17 Source: US Federal Reserve, Sept. 30, 2025, based on the percentage of credit card loans at commercial banks that are delinquent.
  • 18 Source: US Bureau of Economic Analysis, Aug. 31, 2025, based on the year-over-year percent change in the core Personal Consumption Expenditure.
  • 19 Source: Bloomberg L.P., Oct. 15, 2025, based on the US Dollar Index, which measures the value of the US dollar versus a trade-weighted basket of currencies. The US dollar increased by 28% from the middle of 2014 through the end of 2016.
  • 20 Source: Bloomberg L.P., Oct. 15, 2025, based on the 13.31% peak-to-trough decline in the fall of 2015 following the Fed rate hike in 2015.
  • 21 Source: Bloomberg L.P., Oct. 15, 2025, based on the 3-year US Treasury inflation breakeven. A breakeven inflation rate is a market-derived estimate of future inflation, calculated by comparing the yield on a standard government bond (nominal) to the yield on a Treasury Inflation-Protected Security (TIPS) of the same maturity.
  • 22 Source: US Bureau of Labor Statistics, Aug. 31, 2025.
  • 23 Source: Bloomberg L.P., Oct. 15, 2025. Year to date, the gold spot price has risen by 59.93% and bitcoin has risen 17.21%.
  • 24 Source: Bloomberg L.P., Oct. 15, 2025, based on the US Dollar Index, which measures the value of the US dollar versus a trade-weighted basket of currencies. The US dollar declined 11.99% from its 2025 peak on Jan. 13, 2025 to its 2025 trough on July 2, 2025. Since the 2025 trough, the US dollar has increased 2.11%.
  • 25 Source: Bloomberg L.P., Oct. 15, 2025, based on the 10-year US Treasury rate, which peaked at 4.79% on Jan. 14, 2025 and closed at 4.03% on Oct. 14, 2025.
  • 26 Source: Bloomberg L.P., Oct. 14, 2025, based on the 14.10% year-to-date return of the S&P 500 Index.
  • 27 Source: US Bureau of Labor Statistics, Aug. 31, 2025, based on nonfarm payrolls.
  • 28 Source: Institute for Supply Management, Sept. 30, 2025, based on the ISM Manufacturing Index, which is based on Institute of Supply Management surveys of manufacturing firms in the US, which monitors employment, production, inventories, new orders, and supplier deliveries.
  • 29 Source: US Census Bureau, Aug. 31, 2025, based on US New Privately Owned Housing Units Started by Structure.

Important information

NA4911827

Image: Rapeepong Puttakumwong / Getty

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

Alpha refers to the excess returns of a fund relative to the return of a benchmark index.

A basis point is one-hundredth of a percentage point.

Bitcoins are considered a highly speculative investment due to their lack of guaranteed value and limited track record. Because of their digital nature, they pose risks from hackers, malware, fraud, and operational glitches. Bitcoins aren't legal tender and are operated by a decentralized authority, unlike government-issued currencies. Bitcoin exchanges and bitcoin accounts aren't backed or insured by any type of federal or government program or bank.

The Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index measures the investment grade, fixed-rate, taxable corporate bond market. It includes US dollar-denominated securities publicly issued by US and non-US industrial, utility, and financial issuers.

Cash flow is the net amount of cash and cash equivalents generated by a business.

Commodities may subject an investor to greater volatility than traditional securities such as stocks and bonds, and can fluctuate significantly based on weather, political, tax, and other regulatory and market developments.

Credit risk is the risk of default on a debt that may arise from a borrower or issuer of bonds failing to make required payments.

Credit spread is the difference in yield between bonds of similar maturity but with different credit quality.

Deflation is a decrease in the price level of goods and services.

Diversification does not guarantee a profit or eliminate the risk of loss.

Monetary easing refers to the lowering of interest rates and deposit ratios by central banks.

Gross domestic product (GDP) is a broad indicator of a region’s economic activity, measuring the monetary value of all the finished goods and services produced in that region over a specified time period.

Forward earnings per share is a variant of earnings per share and is calculated using a company’s projected earnings over the next 12 months divided by the number of outstanding shares.

Fluctuations in the price of gold and precious metals may affect the profitability of companies in the gold and precious metals sector. Changes in the political or economic conditions of countries where companies in the gold and precious metals sector are located may have a direct effect on the price of gold and precious metals.

An investment cannot be made into an index.

Inflation is the rate at which the general price level for goods and services is increasing.

Breakeven inflation is the difference in yield between a nominal Treasury security and a Treasury Inflation-Protected Security of the same maturity.

The price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio measures a stock’s valuation by dividing its share price by its earnings per share.

Purchasing Managers’ Indexes (PMI) are based on monthly surveys of companies worldwide and gauge business conditions within the manufacturing and services sectors.

A risk asset is generally described as any financial security or instrument that carries risk and is likely to fluctuate in price.

The S&P 500® Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the US stock market.

A spot price is the current market price at which an asset is bought or sold for immediate payment and delivery.

Spread represents the difference between two values or asset returns.

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic and political conditions.

Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) are US Treasury securities that are indexed to inflation.

The US Dollar Index measures the value of the US dollar relative to the majority of its most significant trading partners.

West Texas Intermediate (WTI) is a type of light, sweet crude oil that comes from the US.

Yield spread is the difference between yields on differing debt instruments, calculated by deducting the yield of one instrument from another.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of Oct. 16, 2025. These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties, and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

Above The Noise: Has The ‘Bubble Talk’ Been Blown Out Of Proportion? by Invesco US

data.title: Arqit Quantum: Overvalued, Hype-Driven, And With High Cash Burn
data.description:
Arqit Quantum stock investment thesis

Today's news about the U.S. government potentially acquiring equity stakes in major quantum companies has inspired me to update my Arqit Quantum Inc. (NASDAQ:ARQQ) thesis. Which companies are actually subject to this potential equity stake is still unclear at this time. Although some firms were mentioned in news articles, Reuters writes: "The Commerce Department denies current negotiations with quantum firms."

As has been the case in the entire sector for some time now, there is a lot of hype here as well with this news. And given that Arqit is still extremely highly valued and the cash burn has improved but is still present, I consider Arqit to be overvalued.

What were my previous articles about Arqit like?

I wrote my first article about Arqit in November 2024. At the time, I was interested in the company because it is active in the field of quantum security, which I think will be important in the future. Arqit's plan is to differentiate itself from the competition by offering software-based quantum security rather than hardware-based quantum security. This means that potential customers do not need specialized equipment, and it should, in theory, be more cost-effective for them.

But I think it is currently unclear which approach will prevail in the end. Arqit's software for symmetric key agreements, which uses endpoint key generation, is appealing because it limits the risk of the keys being intercepted. But hardware solutions also have their place and could ultimately be safer than software solutions.

In my last article about Arqit, which was published in May this year, I put forward the thesis that the second half of the year would probably be better than the first in terms of revenues. In the first half of the year, Arqit generated $67k in revenue, but they emphasized that a multiyear contract was signed, which should increase sales in the second half of the year.

As expected, the preliminary results for H2/25 and FY25 show that revenues have increased significantly thanks to the multi-year contract. Revenues between $525,000 and $535,000 for FY25 are certainly a big step forward.

All the more pleasing is the fact that this multi-year contract will probably lead to sales in the 2026 financial year rising by more than 100% to $1.2 million.

ARQQ's balance sheet

Both Arqit's net income and FCF are at nearly -$25 million in the TTM comparison. So this will be approximately the amount of cash burn. However, it should be noted that this figure is trending upward, as it was previously roughly -$45 million.

But Arqit's cash position is only $36.9 million as of September 2025. So I see a strong need for fresh capital. Although the business appears to be secure for the next 12 months, there does not seem to be much room for error. I mean depending on how the margins develop with the new contract, the cash burn could also increase again.

But in such a case, the current hype surrounding quantum stocks is advantageous, as it helps to issue more shares to raise fresh capital. And as can be seen from the number of shares outstanding, these have increased significantly in the last year.

Given this, I would not be surprised if new shares were issued in the future to strengthen the cash position and, unfortunately, dilute existing shareholders.

What do I think the future holds for Arqit?

I think the most important application areas for Quantum Security will be in the military and in securing important utility companies. Therefore, it will probably be very important to see which companies secure the largest contracts in this area. To participate here, Arqit has joined the Oracle (ORCL) Defense Ecosystem and introduced the SKA Edge Controller, a product tailored to military operations. And the fact that the US government is allegedly considering acquiring shares in quantum companies also points in this direction.

I think it is highly likely that the first applications will be for the military, followed by those for private users or companies. GPS, for example, was used for military purposes long before it was used for civilian purposes.

ARQQ stock valuation

The valuations of quantum companies are currently extremely high. Due to their low sales and current stage of development, many of them resemble moonshot bets from a venture capital playbook. The only company with an apparently low valuation is SealSQ (LAES), with a PS multiple of 37x. Arqit, on the other hand, is the second most expensive company of the peer group, with a PS ratio of 2,000x. But it should be noted that Arqit forward multiple is significantly lower, as the company is expected to generate $1.2 million in revenue by FY26. This gives the shares a forward multiple of ~445x, based on a market cap of roughly $530m.

Obviously, this multiple is also extremely high, leaving no room for a safety margin. This is why I consider the stock to be overvalued, as I also don't see a clear competitive advantage at this time.

Risks

The biggest risks are probably that the software will be unreliable, or that insufficient funds will be available for investment in research and development, or mergers and acquisitions, to enable further growth. On the other hand, I also believe that it could give companies a significant competitive advantage once they have established a presence in the military sector and obtained the necessary authorizations. This creates the risk that a competitor will receive the necessary authorizations first.

Conclusion

A few days ago, I published an article about IonQ (IONQ) in which I argued that the company is overvalued and its competitors are in a stronger position. IonQ, however, has a PS multiple of "only" 271x, and I see the company as being much closer to commercial market readiness than Arqit. I mean, IonQ is still one of the market leaders even though I think Alphabet (GOOG) is in a better position.

Therefore, I can only view Arqit as overvalued, as its current market position does not justify the current valuation. Of course, the company can grow into its valuation, but to do so, Arqit's sales would need to grow by triple digits every year for the next few years. That is a very difficult task.

Not to mention that the balance sheet and cash reserves do not offer much downside protection. And if there are no positive free cash flows in the near future and no additional sources of fresh capital, the situation could become problematic in terms of liquidity.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 3 Stocks Powering Data Center Growth
data.description:
Data Center Growth Set to Explode

The term “data centers,” like “hyperscalers” and “semiconductors,” has become ubiquitous as the AI revolution takes hold. Data centers are the centerpiece of the physical AI footprint, housing the computational equipment like servers and GPUs to run its applications. The global market is forecasted to grow at a CAGR of 25.6% through 2034, and McKinsey projects that AI workloads will account for 70% of data center demand by 2030.

Source: Polaris Market Research

AWS Outage: Data Centers in the Hot Seat

Maintaining uninterrupted operations of data centers has become more critical than ever, as these systems are central to much of the digital world. On Oct. 20, a major outage at Amazon Web Services (AWS) disrupted services like Venmo, Alexa, and Coinbase, among others. The incident showcased the growing role of data centers in our everyday lives and underscored the urgent need to strengthen their resilience. Losses from the outage are estimated to amount to billions.

The Data Center Ecosystem

While investment opportunities in chips and utilities have dominated much of the headlines, some data center-related products and services have remained overlooked. Data center spending is so pervasive that it's expected to surpass spending on traditional office buildings this year.

Source Link: NY Times

Below, I’ve outlined three sub-sectors that are centered around data center operations.

1. Data Center Operators

Data center operators own and manage the facilities that house servers and storage systems that keep AI applications running. Operators will then lease space to enterprises, cloud providers, and hyperscalers, providing physical locations to deploy their computing hardware, while some companies choose to build their own private data centers.

2. Thermal Management

The HVAC industry has exploded as AI chips generate significantly more heat per rack, which are the metal frames that hold servers. Modern AI racks often exceed 50-100 kilowatts of power consumption compared with the historical 5-10 kilowatts for normal computing. This has made the need for scalable, energy-efficient cooling solutions increasingly critical.

3. Data Center-Specific Software

Software and data management have also become requisite for data center operations, enabling monitoring and maintenance across facilities. These systems allow operators to remotely adjust cooling parameters, balance power loads, and monitor operations to minimize interruptions. Data center software also enables interoperability between the enterprises storing data and the data centers themselves.

4. Satellite and Communications Data Processing

I recently wrote an article about satellite communications. Beyond telecommunications, satellites now power military, government, and environmental systems that generate massive amounts of real-time data. Data centers are increasingly tasked with managing the complex web of satellite networks, and industry leaders are scaling their data center infrastructure accordingly.

While we've covered the AI revolution from multiple angles, including utilities powering these facilities to the chip stocks enabling them, we wanted to explore some potentially overlooked stocks related to the sprawling data center ecosystem that continues to expand.

3 Stocks Powering Data Center Growth

SA Quant has an excellent track record in identifying companies in AI-adjacent sectors with strong return potential. Nearly a year ago, we identified four Top Utilities Stocks Powering the AI Revolution, and since publication, those stocks have returned an average of 56%, handily beating the S&P 500 and broader utilities sector.

Top Utilities Stocks Powering the AI Revolution vs. XLU vs. SPY (Oct. 25, 2024 - Oct. 22, 2025)

Just over six weeks ago, I added four more AI infrastructure stocks, three of which have gained more than 20%, compared to the S&P 500’s 3.45% return over the same period.

Backstage Pass: Top 4 AI Infrastructure Stocks vs. SPY (Aug. 28, 2025 - Oct. 22, 2025)

We hope to continue the trend with the three data-center-specific companies outlined below.

1. Applied Digital Corporation (APLD)

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Internet Services and Infrastructure

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/23/2025): 7 out of 540

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/23/2025): 1 out of 21

  • Market Capitalization: $8.56B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

Applied Digital Corporation is a data center operator with campuses in North Dakota that designs and builds facilities used to store the equipment for AI and blockchain applications. APLD’s ‘A+’ Growth has been driven by long-term, multi-billion-dollar lease agreements, including a new $5 billion deal with an investment-grade hyperscaler. The company showcases 125% FWD EBITDA Growth vs. the sector’s 11.13% and an operating cash flow growth of 61.63%.

Source: Q1 FY2026 Investor Presentation

While Applied Digital’s earnings remain negative, the company’s financial trajectory is improving as major data center contracts begin to ramp up.

“With the CoreWeave lease supporting roughly half a billion in annual net operating income and Polaris Forge 2 poised to significantly increase that figure, we are laying the foundation to reach our stated goal of $1 billion of NOI run rate within five years. And this is just the beginning,” said APLD CEO West Cummins.

APLD is simultaneously experiencing improving valuation and accelerating momentum. The company's valuation grade improved from an ‘F’ three months ago to a ‘D+’ today. In the same period, the stock has returned nearly 200%.

APLD Momentum Grade

Applied Digital's strategic presence in North Dakota positions the company to capitalize on cheap and abundant energy, low construction and operational costs, and a favorable climate to keep its data centers cool. These factors, combined with the company’s excellent fundamentals and strong momentum, make APLD a solid data center stock.

2. Pure Storage, Inc. (PSTG)

  • Sector: Information Technology
